cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out the answer to this question. The question is asking why Babbage failed to continue his research work in 1828. Let me read through the passage again to make sure I understand the context. The passage mentions that after returning to London in 1828, Babbage was "at the end of his resources," many bills remained unpaid, his assistant and co-workers quarreled with him and left with expensive tools. For one year, no work was done. Then, during that period, he had a new idea but the government wouldn't help. The options are A) poor health, B) ran out of money, C) co-workers argued and left, D) spent money on bills. The key points in the passage: He was at the end of his resources (which suggests money), unpaid bills, co-workers left. The question is about the main reason. The passage says "his health broke down" happened before, leading to a long holiday abroad. Then in 1828, the main issues are money ("at the end of his resources"), unpaid bills, and co-workers leaving. The question is why he failed to continue research in 1828. The passage says that during the period when he had no work done (after returning in 1828), he had no money, co-workers left, and bills unpaid. But the question asks for the main reason. The answer options include B) he almost ran out of money. The passage states he was "at the end of his resources," which is a direct reference to money. While the co-workers argued and left, that's presented as a consequence of the financial issues perhaps, or a contributing factor. The main reason would be the lack of money. Also, the fact that he couldn't pay bills suggests financial strain.
